text: It wouldn’t help against bots, but it was something . After braiding her hair, Lara wrapped her head with a swath of cloth, leaving a portion loose to cover her face. She tucked the canteen into the fabric tied around her waist, picked up her bag, and went outside, sliding the door back into place behind her. Not that it mattered. If someone wanted to steal from her home, there was nothing stopping them. She kept her few belongings of any value with her at all times The morning sky was yellowish-gray, the air already warm. The sun blazed through the haze on the eastern horizon. It would be another hot day . Other people were stirring as she walked southward down the cracked dirt path. Some of them were covered in cloth, like Lara, ready to head out of town to scavenge the ruins. The seamstress had opened the panel on the front of her shack, already working with precise, delicate hands on someone’s clothing. Hal, the cook, had his fire pit going, thin strips of meat sizzling on the grill over the top of it . The food smelled delicious, but Lara hadn’t contributed, and didn’t have much of anything to barter with. She wouldn’t get rid of the canteen for a few mouthfuls of food. It was worth more than that . She hurried along, ignoring the twisting pain in her stomach. Already, people sat outside their shacks, quietly staring at passersby. Lara didn’t meet their gazes; she was too close to becoming like them — folk who didn’t want to deal with the bots and had chosen to give up, instead. Many of them were missing fingers or hands, result of Warlord’s justice , and their eyes were dead. Too much like those mechanical monsters for Lara’s comfort . If you didn’t want to trade with bots — whether with scrap or more personal wares — for food, seeds, and goods, you had to work even harder to survive. Why bother, when death was so likely ? “ Lara !” She cast a sidelong glance at the man approaching her and somehow kept from walking faster. She’d hoped to be gone before he rose . “What do you want, Devon?” she asked, continuing toward the water pump near the center of the settlement . “A ‘good morning’ wouldn’t hurt.” Devon fell into step beside her . “Good morning, and goodbye. I have work to do .” “You wouldn’t have to if you let me help you .” “I don’t want your help .” He caught her elbow, forcing her to stop. She glanced at his hand, eyes narrowed, and then glared at him . “It’d be better than digging through shit every day, hoping to find something to trade for some food.” He leaned closer, gaze dipping. “I have plenty to share. You’ll fill out in no time .” It wasn’t unusual for women to latch onto men who could provide. Devon grew some of his own food, owned a chicken, and had connections in the Market. He wasn’t bad looking, either. Long, wavy blond hair down to his shoulders, rich brown eyes, and strong features. Likely the cleanest man in the district. Many women had cozied up to him, and had been grateful for it . “I’d be a gentle lover, Lara,” he whispered, brushing a finger along her jaw . Lara would not be one of those women . She yanked her arm from his grasp and stepped back. “I’d rather starve .” “Oh, come on! What are you trying to prove? Why not take what I have to offer ?” “Why not be your whore, you mean ?” “Better than being a bot-banger, like your sister .” Lara flinched, gritting her teeth. Fists clenched, she turned away from him and walked to the pump . “You won’t get many chances, Lara!” Devon called as she rinsed and filled the canteen. “You’re not the only woman worth looking at here .” She screwed on the cap and bit her tongue. Rage burned in her chest, mixed with shame. They all knew about Tabitha. Why wouldn’t they assume Lara would trade her body for food, too? She didn’t allow herself to look back as she hurried south . Her heart finally slowed as she passed the last shacks on the outskirts of town and stepped onto the main road into the southside ruins. A few other scavengers were already out, moving in different directions down some of the other paths. She knew them all by name, though they kept their faces covered. Scavengers typically gave each other wide berth; conflicts weren’t worth anyone’s time . The wind whipped at her clothing while she returned to the site she’d scavenged the day before. Finding the pitcher was encouraging. There could be more to find there, with a little luck and some hard work . She paused at the spot, turning to look over the desolate landscape once again. Dark clouds dominated the southern horizon. Between the ruins and those clouds waited the endless Dust. People talked about the scrap that could be found out there, buried in the dirt; scrap that only dustwalkers went to find. But they were an odd lot. Tabitha had called them crazy, and Lara agreed . The sun was over the horizon now, casting its sickly yellow glow through the haze. There wasn’t any more time to waste. Lara crouched and dug through the rubble, working till her hands ached and her fingers were raw, stopping twice to pluck splinters from her skin. Old paper crumbled in her hands, and every gust of wind blew dirt in her face . The day wore on, uncaring for the struggles of mankind . By afternoon, her sweat and blood had only earned her a few more bits of rusted metal and some cracked plastic. Combined with her haul from the prior day, it would put food in her belly, but not much. Sighing,
